---
title: "Prospect Dev Salary Snapshot 2025"
author: Greg Brooks
date: 04/25/2026
draft: true
categories:
    - Industry Commentary
---

Salary research for prospect dev roles curated from LinkedIn, public databases, and more.

***

[](../../images/cash2.jpg){width=50%}

# Overview
#### The Bad News
This is substantially worse than the [Apra International Salary Survey](https://aprahome.org/Resources/Salary-Survey){target="_blank"}. If you want stronger methodology, more comprehensive data (e.g. demographics), and mild abuse of the "3d" effect for bar charts (yuck) - the report they commission from the Smithbucklin corp. has got you covered. Though, if my math is right, we won't see another survey from them until 2027.[^paywall] This blog post is basically napkin math in comparison.

#### The Good News
My little snapshot is free to explore via dashboard on this blog, or download as .csv. This was a natural extension to my research on prospect dev job descriptions, and it fits with my mission to be an independent source of information about my field. I also think my analyses potentially pull in data that is publicly available, but was not gathered by the Apra Survey. 

# Intro
Given the 14%-31% response rate of the Apra survey, I figured I might be able to dig up salaries that are publicly posted (e.g. by government websites) that potentially don't get collected within the Apra survey. It should make for interesting comparison, at the very least.

#### Scope
My research is decidedly more narrow in scope, placing a priority on run-of-the-mill analyst jobs in prospect dev. Focusing less on management and other fundraising operations. 

Last year, I pulled 430 fundraising-related job titles from the [CMAP dataset](../../field_notes/Creating_Jobs_Dataset/index.qmd), and at least 150 prospect dev job titles as a subset. So my independent analyses are actually not far off from the scale of the Apra Survey, in terms of sample size. If we combine the "Relationship / Prospect Managment" and "Prospect Research" respondents of Apra's 2024 report, we end up with ~446 people. 

#### 




***

# My reading list to learn more
- [Performance-Based Salary Distribution in Universities Using Extreme Gradient Boosting with Particle Swarm Optimization](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/abstract/document/11341436)
- [Education vs. Earnings: An Analytical Perspective on Fields of Study and Salaries Using Python](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-95652-2_31)
- 

***

# References & Sources
- OpenGovPay.com
- GovSalaries.com
- LinkedIn Job Alerts
- Subhani, S., Memon, S.A. & AlShebli, B. CMap: a database for mapping job titles, sector specialization, and promotions across 24 sectors. Sci Data 12, 1214 (2025). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-025-05526-3


[^paywall]: I'm also curious who actually pays the $275 non-member fee to access the salary survey...which seems too steep to be realistic as an actual product people would buy. I'm guessing that number was chosen to make the Professional Membership benefits seem all that more attractive, a [psychological pricing technique](https://www.advergize.com/glossary/psychological-pricing/){target="_blank"} that nudges users toward the ongoing subscription. Presumably, though, there are some folks in prospect dev who don't have access to the Professional Membership, which is another reason to have independent and free data for all.
